fast.summary: Allegro.com built a unified Two-Tower recommendation system that serves three distinct use cases—similar products, complementary items, and inspirational discovery—using the same model architecture with only minor component changes. The system uses text and structured product attributes with Approximate Nearest Neighbour search and was validated through two years of A/B testing.
fast.key_idea: A single Two-Tower retrieval model can address multiple recommendation intents (similarity, complementarity, inspiration) by swapping only a few components in the model or serving layer, dramatically reducing maintenance overhead at scale.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners can replace multiple bespoke recommendation pipelines with one unified architecture, cutting engineering costs while maintaining strong performance across diverse placement types in large-scale e-commerce settings.
possible_use_cases: Building a single retrieval backbone that powers 'similar items', 'frequently bought together', and 'you might also like' placements without retraining separate models
Scaling product recommendations over highly dynamic catalogues using ANN search on dense embeddings derived from text and structured attributes
Reducing MLOps complexity by standardizing recommendation infrastructure across desktop and mobile channels under one deployable architecture

deep.method_breakdown: The system uses a Two-Tower neural network where separate encoder towers independently embed query and candidate products using textual and structured attributes into a shared latent space. At inference time, candidate embeddings are pre-computed and indexed for Approximate Nearest Neighbour (ANN) search, enabling sub-linear retrieval at scale. Three distinct recommendation tasks (similarity, complementarity, inspiration) are served by the same backbone architecture with task-specific modifications to loss functions, training data construction, or post-retrieval filtering logic.
deep.key_innovation: The key novelty is a single unified Two-Tower architecture that serves three semantically distinct recommendation intents — suggest (similar items), complement (co-purchase/co-need items), and inspire (discovery/serendipity) — by swapping only minimal components rather than maintaining separate models per placement. This contrasts with prior industry systems that typically deploy task-specific retrieval models, significantly reducing engineering and maintenance overhead across dozens of placement surfaces.
deep.technical_insight: Representing products purely via textual and structured attributes (rather than collaborative filtering signals like clicks or purchases) is the critical design choice: it makes the system robust to catalogue churn (new/cold-start items have no interaction history but always have metadata), which is essential for a highly dynamic e-commerce catalogue. This content-based approach also allows zero-shot generalization to new placements without retraining, since the embedding space is grounded in item semantics rather than co-occurrence statistics.
deep.limitations: Content-based retrieval inherently struggles to capture latent user preference signals that are not expressed in product attributes (e.g., brand affinity, price sensitivity, trending items), so it may underperform compared to interaction-based models for warm items with rich behavioral data. The unified architecture also introduces tension between tasks — objectives for complementarity and similarity are often contradictory in embedding space, and a shared model may sacrifice per-task optimality for architectural simplicity. ANN index staleness for a highly dynamic catalogue can also degrade retrieval quality if re-indexing cadence is insufficient.
interview_questions: How do you train the Two-Tower model to differentiate between similarity, complementarity, and inspiration objectives using the same architecture?
How do you handle the cold-start problem for newly listed products in the candidate tower at retrieval time?
What loss functions or negative sampling strategies did you use, and how did they differ across the three recommendation tasks?
How do you keep the ANN index fresh given the highly dynamic product catalogue at Allegro's scale?
What were the main failure modes observed during A/B testing, and how did you diagnose whether issues stemmed from retrieval quality versus ranking?
interview_answers: The same tower encoders are reused across tasks, but training data construction and loss supervision differ: for similarity the positive pairs are semantically related items, for complementarity positives come from co-purchase or cart co-occurrence signals, and for inspiration positives may be drawn from broader browsing sessions. Task-specific objectives shape what the shared embedding space captures without requiring separate model weights.

Because the candidate tower relies solely on product attributes (text, category, structured fields) rather than interaction history, any new product can be immediately embedded and indexed as soon as its catalogue metadata is available — there is no cold-start barrier on the item side. The query tower similarly operates on item attributes, so the system is fully content-based and catalogue-dynamic by design.

Contrastive or softmax-based in-batch negative sampling is the standard approach for Two-Tower models; for complementarity the key challenge is distinguishing hard negatives (same category but not complementary) from true positives, often requiring popularity-corrected sampling to avoid frequency bias. The paper highlights modifying training data construction as the primary lever rather than changing the loss architecture itself.

The typical industry approach — and likely used here — is periodic batch re-embedding of the full catalogue and ANN index rebuilds on a scheduled cadence (e.g., hourly or daily), with the frequency tuned to the rate of catalogue change. The system's content-based design helps because changed or new items only require a forward pass through the frozen candidate tower, not a full model retrain.

In Two-Tower systems, retrieval recall (whether the right candidates are in the top-K) is the primary diagnostic: low engagement can indicate the ANN index is returning irrelevant candidates regardless of downstream ranker quality. A/B tests comparing retrieval-only versus full pipeline help isolate where gains or regressions originate, and offline recall@K metrics against held-out interaction data serve as a proxy signal before online experiments.
